title: Andean plying
subtitle: Winding a singles skein onto your hand to ply two strands from one bobbin without a lazy kate
excerpt: Andean plying winds a singles yarn into a specific figure-8 configuration on the hand, creating a plying bracelet with two ends: one from the outer layer and one from the inner. Plying from both ends produces a balanced two-ply yarn from a single bobbin, with no lazy kate needed. It is particularly suited to single small skeins and to situations where the spinning equipment is limited.
sourceNotes: Andean plying is named for Andean spindle practice where the method originated. Modern wheel-spinning use of the method is widely documented in guild manuals. Description here is observation-led.

content.text: is wound from a single source (one bobbin or a small centre-pull ball) and slid off the hand as a coil. Both ends of the coil are drawn together for plying, producing a
when the plying twist matches the singles twist. The method works for quantities up to about 50 g; larger amounts become unwieldy on the hand.

items.name: Small bobbin or centre-pull ball of Z-twist singles
Spinning wheel or drop spindle (for plying)

content.text: Hold your non-dominant hand out, fingers pointing up. Drape the singles end over your little finger, then bring it across the back of the hand and loop it around the index finger. Bring it back across the palm and around the little finger again. Continue in this figure-8 pattern (little finger to index finger and back) until all the singles are wound. Keep tension consistent as you wind. Tuck the final end under a wrap to hold it.

Slide the wound coil off your hand slowly and carefully, maintaining the figure-8 twist. You will have two ends free: the end you started winding from and the end you tucked in last. Hold both ends together and let the coil rest on your wrist or hang from the two ends.

Ply the two ends together as a standard two-ply, treadling in S-twist. The coil will unwind naturally as you draw from it. Keep both strands under even tension throughout. When the coil is nearly exhausted, slow down: the last few metres can tangle if wound off too quickly.
Wind the finished ply onto the niddy-noddy. Soak in lukewarm water, squeeze out, snap to set the twist, and dry hanging without tension.

items.fix: Wind with even, consistent tension and keep the figure-8 pattern strict. After sliding off, do not let the coil collapse.
items.cause: Coil was wound unevenly or too loosely, allowing strands to slip
items.problem: The coil tangles or knots when pulled from

items.fix: Apply fewer treadle strokes per length of ply drawn. Test a short length: let it hang in a loop from one finger; a balanced ply hangs straight.
items.cause: Too much S-twist applied during plying relative to the singles' Z-twist
items.problem: The finished ply is kinked and over-twisted

items.fix: Wind with more consistent tension. A small difference is normal; when one end runs short, tie the remaining length of the other end onto a separate small bobbin and ply normally.
items.cause: Winding tension was uneven, making one wrap longer than the other
items.problem: The two strands run out at different rates
